title: Stanhope Aspinwall

infobox.value: **British *[Chargé d'affaires](/wiki/Charg%C3%A9_d%27affaires "Chargé d'affaires")* to the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")**  1742–1746 | Succeeded by Sir [James Porter](/wiki/James_Porter_(diplomat) "James Porter (diplomat)"), Ambassador 1746-1762

(born 5 July 1713 in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), [England](/wiki/England "England") and died on 17 January 1771) was a British diplomat. He was born to Richard Aspinwall and his wife Elizabeth Stanhope, the great granddaughter of [Philip Stanhope, 1st Earl of Chesterfield](/wiki/Philip_Stanhope,_1st_Earl_of_Chesterfield "Philip Stanhope, 1st Earl of Chesterfield"), the granddaughter of [Arthur Stanhope](/wiki/Arthur_Stanhope "Arthur Stanhope") and daughter of Charles Stanhope.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

[*Letters of Philip Dormer Stanhope, Earl of Chesterfield*, edited by Lord Mahon: Volume II; London; Richard Bentley 1847; Note on p.261](http://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?u=1&num=261&seq=59&view=image&size=100&id=mdp.39015014180023)

[*The London Magazine: Or, Gentleman's Monthly Intelligencer, 1752*: Volume 21: August: p. 385: *Promotions, Civil and Military*](https://books.google.com/books?id=glEDAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA385)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British diplomat (1713–1771)

**Stanhope Aspinwall** (born 5 July 1713 in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), [England](/wiki/England "England") and died on 17 January 1771) was a British diplomat. He was born to Richard Aspinwall and his wife Elizabeth Stanhope, the great granddaughter of [Philip Stanhope, 1st Earl of Chesterfield](/wiki/Philip_Stanhope,_1st_Earl_of_Chesterfield "Philip Stanhope, 1st Earl of Chesterfield"), the granddaughter of [Arthur Stanhope](/wiki/Arthur_Stanhope "Arthur Stanhope") and daughter of Charles Stanhope.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Stanhope Aspinwall was educated at [Westminster School](/wiki/Westminster_School "Westminster School") from 1722 until at least 1725.

From November 1742 Aspinwall was stationed at [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") in the British Embassy to the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). In his will dated 14 April 1747, he describes himself as 'Chancellor and Secretary of the British Embassy at Constantinople', and under [Everard Fawkener](/wiki/Everard_Fawkener "Everard Fawkener"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) until February 1747 [*Chargé d'affaires*](/wiki/List_of_diplomats_from_the_United_Kingdom_to_the_Ottoman_Empire "List of diplomats from the United Kingdom to the Ottoman Empire") to the Levant Company's embassy. Apparently Fawkener had left the Embassy in a disgruntled state, purportedly for personal reasons. Without an appropriate royal commission, Aspinwall served until the arrival of [Sir James Porter](/wiki/James_Porter_(diplomat) "James Porter (diplomat)")[[3]](#cite_note-3)

On 8 August 1752, [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") appointed Stanhope Aspinwall as his agent and consul general to [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers").[[4]](#cite_note-4) His last appointment was as secretary to [Simon Harcourt, 1st Earl Harcourt](/wiki/Simon_Harcourt,_1st_Earl_Harcourt "Simon Harcourt, 1st Earl Harcourt"), [Ambassador to France](/wiki/List_of_ambassadors_of_Great_Britain_to_France "List of ambassadors of Great Britain to France"), which position Aspinwall was holding when he died on 17 January 1771.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Stanhope Aspinwall’s will dated 14 April 1747 shows that he was married to ‘Magdalena’ (other name ‘Baptistina’) and that he then had two children. One of them Elizabeth later married Sir [William Neville Hart](/wiki/William_Neville_Hart "William Neville Hart").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Two daughters Cecilia Frances and Margaret Catherine were baptised at [St Marylebone Parish Church](/wiki/St_Marylebone_Parish_Church "St Marylebone Parish Church") in [London](/wiki/London "London") in 1751 and 1754 respectively, both presumably born after Aspinwall left [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"). Magdalena survived him. On 21 May 1771, in the [Prerogative Court](/wiki/Prerogative_Court "Prerogative Court") of Canterbury, Administration (with the Will annexed) were granted to her.

Stanhope Aspinwall also translated from French into English *[Rodogune](/wiki/Rodogune "Rodogune"), or, The rival brothers*: a tragedy by [Pierre Corneille](/wiki/Pierre_Corneille "Pierre Corneille") (1606–1684), It was published in London in 1765.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
